1:
The chapter opens with a report brought to David that the Philistines were plundering the threshing-floors of Keilah, and the witnesses read the notice on three levels — providential, geographical, and moral. The grammatical-historical commentators set the frame. Keil and Delitzsch take the whole episode as a specimen of God's dealing with his servant: the Lord gives David fresh pledges that he shall be king, yet plunges him into deeper and deeper trouble to refine him and train him to be a king after his own heart. Saul's massacre at Nob had driven the high priest into David's camp, so that David now carried with him the "light and right" of the ephod, and this Philistine raid furnished the very opportunity to show himself to the people as their deliverer. Ellicott notes that it was for just such a duty that the prophet Gad had summoned David back into Judah (1 Sam. 22:5), Saul having grown so absorbed in David's imaginary crimes that he neglected the higher royal task of guarding his people. He places Keilah in the lowlands of Judah near the Philistine frontier, south of Adullam and overlooking the valley of Elah; Clarke fixes it as a fortified town on the road to Hebron, and explains the robbing of the threshing-floors as an old raiders' trick — falling on the corn once it lay gathered in the open field, as in Gideon's day (Judg. 6:4).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the moral. David, though hunted and with only a handful of men, is "forward enough" to come to Keilah's relief; concerned for his country's safety, he "bravely abhorred the thought of sacrificing the common welfare to his private revenge." In this he renders good for evil and becomes a type of him who not only ventured but laid down his life for his enemies. Henry states the political maxim twice for emphasis: the way for any country to be quiet is to let God's church be quiet in it, for "if Saul fight against David, the Philistines shall fight against his country." The wesleyan-arminian Wesley agrees exactly, tracing the incursion to the departure of God and David from Saul, and warning princes who persecute God's people and ministers to expect nothing but vexation on all sides.

The reformed Gill contents himself with the plain sense — that either the men of Keilah or well-wishers of their country carried the tidings of their distress. Beneath the differences of emphasis the witnesses are one: the same providence that was disciplining David was also arming him for public service, and his willingness to shield an ungrateful land already marks him out as the man after God's own heart.
2:
Threatened Keilah lay open before him, yet David would not stir without asking God, and the witnesses fasten on two things: the nobility of the inquiry and the manner of it. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke is warmest on the first. Had David merely loved himself, he says, he would have welcomed this Philistine invasion of Judah as a happy diversion, embroiling the enemy who hunted him; most men with his wrongs would have joined their country's foes to avenge their private adversities. Instead "he thinks of nothing but succouring Keilah" — a rare instance of disinterested heroism — and once God has spoken, "when God promises success, who need be afraid of the face of any enemy?" The reformed Gill notes the real grounds of David's hesitation: he had no commission from Saul his king, and his force was small; he did well, therefore, to lay the case before the Lord.

The grammatical-historical commentators dispute how the answer was obtained, and this is the chapter's first genuine division. Keil and Delitzsch state flatly that David inquired "through the Urim and Thummim of the high priest." Ellicott resists this: the ephod, he insists, was not yet with David, for the narrative expressly notes that Abiathar joined him only at Keilah (v. 6); the inquiry must therefore have been made through Gad the prophet, who was with him. He shows that such prophetic inquiries used the very formula found here, as when Jehoshaphat inquired of Micaiah (1 Kings 22:5, 7–8), and cites the Talmud's distinction that a prophet's decree is revocable while the decision of the Urim and Thummim is irrevocable. Gill leans the same way, judging that the inquiry was very probably by Gad, since Abiathar had not yet come; though he records that Kimchi and others read it of the Urim and Thummim.

Clarke stands with a foot in each camp: he too thinks the inquiry was probably through Abiathar, and would move the sixth verse up to follow the first, so that the ephod is present from the outset. The grammatical-historical Jamieson, Fausset and Brown add only the topographical note that Keilah lay within Judah, about twelve miles south of Jerusalem. The divergence is not idle: it turns on whether the divine reply came by the shining oracle of the breastplate or by the living word of a prophet. But on the substance the witnesses are agreed — David sought God's mind before God's work, and God answered him, "Go, and smite the Philistines, and save Keilah."
3:
David's men recoil at the double danger, and the witnesses, though few here, draw out the psychology of fear and the geography that made it acute. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the protest in its full force: "Behold, here in Judah we are in fear" — that is, not even safe from Saul's pursuit on their own ground — "how much more then if we come to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines?" Two enemies press them at once, one at their back and one before their face; to march west against the Philistine ranks is to expose their flank to the king who hunts them. To infuse courage into such men, Keil observes, David does not overrule them but inquires of the Lord a second time.

Ellicott, also of the grammatical-historical school, clears away a false inference. The words "here in Judah" do not imply that Keilah lay outside Judah's territory; they mean only that the district round Keilah was at that moment under Philistine domination. It is the open country, he notes, that first falls under an invader's control in times of Philistine supremacy, while the walled and fortified places, Keilah among them, hold out much longer. The men's fear, then, was not groundless: they were being asked to leave the relative shelter of the Judean hills and descend into a lowland already overrun by the enemy.

The reformed Gill reads the same anxiety, noting that the men were afraid even in the heart of Judah, their own tribe, where they had many friends, lest Saul and his army should fall upon them and crush them. The witnesses do not divide; together they let us see a little band, newly gathered and still untried, whose hearts failed them at the mere apprehension of Saul, much more at the prospect of a pitched battle with the Philistine host. Their weakness is not concealed but recorded, and it sets in relief both David's steadiness and the patience of a God who condescends to answer a leader burdened with fearful followers. The verse thus prepares for the renewed inquiry of v. 4, in which the Lord meets not David's doubt, for he had none, but his soldiers', with a promise plain enough to animate the greatest coward in the ranks.
4:
David inquires a second time, and the witnesses are unusually united on why — not for his own sake but for his men's. The reformed Gill states it plainly: David "firmly believed it was the will of God he should go and succeed," so the repeated inquiry was made to remove the doubts and fears that hung on his soldiers' minds. The wesleyan-arminian voices say the same in fewer words: Clarke, that "this was to satisfy his men, who made the strong objections mentioned in the preceding verse"; Wesley, that it was "not for his own, but for his soldiers' satisfaction." The point is quietly important. The oracle is not treated as a machine to be worked twice for certainty's sake, but as a pastoral accommodation: the leader who is himself assured stoops to the weakness of those he leads and secures for them a word they cannot doubt.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the fuller answer that met the need. Keil and Delitzsch record that this time God did not merely permit but commanded and promised — "Arise, go down to Keilah; for I will deliver the Philistines into thine hand" — and that David then went, fought, drove off their cattle, inflicted a severe defeat, and delivered the inhabitants. Ellicott marks the tenderness of the arrangement: the second inquiry was made "for the sake of inspiring his little army with confidence before embarking on the seemingly desperate attempt," and, as in the first case, through the prophet Gad, since Abiathar had not yet arrived with the ephod.

Thus the difference between the first answer and the second is a difference of measure, not of meaning. The first gave leave and duty ("Go, and smite the Philistines, and save Keilah"); the second added the express assurance of victory, the very thing a frightened band required. Where the men could see only the ranks of the Philistines in front and the sword of Saul behind, God set a promise between them and their fear. The witnesses agree that this is how faith is strengthened in the weak: not by scolding their timidity but by giving them a firmer word to lean upon, so that, as Henry says elsewhere of this hour, the assurance of the Lord was enough to animate the greatest coward in the regiment. The verse leaves David armed with a double warrant — a commission that needed no leave from Saul, and a pledge that could not fail.
5:
The battle is told in a single crowded verse — David and his men go, fight, carry off the Philistines' cattle, smite them with a great slaughter, and save the inhabitants of Keilah — and the few witnesses draw out its energy and its faith. The reformed Gill fastens on the spring of the whole action: David and his men went to Keilah "animated by a commission from God, and a promise of success by him." The victory is not credited to numbers or valour alone but to the double warrant secured in the preceding verses; a small and lately fearful band prevails because it marches under a word that cannot fail.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke supplies the concrete detail of the spoil. When the text says David "brought away their cattle," it means the forage and plunder which the Philistines had themselves seized, driving the country bare round about Keilah; David turns their own raid against them, recovering by way of reprisal what they had robbed from the threshing-floors. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, in his running account, sums the sequence in order: David "fought against the Philistines, drove off their cattle, inflicted a severe defeat upon them, and thus delivered the inhabitants of Keilah."

Read together, the witnesses let the verse do three things at once. It vindicates the promise of v. 4 — the Lord had said, "I will deliver the Philistines into thine hand," and so it proved. It answers the fear of v. 3 — the men who dreaded the Philistine armies now scatter them and drive off their herds. And it establishes David, still an outlaw with no royal commission, as the true protector of his people, doing the king's neglected work while the king pursues him. There is no controversy among the commentators here; the verse is action, not doctrine, and they are content to mark that the deliverance was complete: the enemy defeated, their booty recovered, and a threatened city set free. What Saul would not do for Keilah, the hunted son of Jesse did — and did it in the strength of the God who had bidden him go. The relief of the town, however, will prove no safety to David, for the very presence that saved Keilah now shuts him, as the next verses show, within a walled place where Saul thinks to trap him.
6:
A brief parenthesis about Abiathar's arrival with the ephod carries more weight than its length, for on it turns the chapter's dispute over how David inquired of the Lord. The wesleyan-arminian voices open it. Clarke, believing the ephod essential to every inquiry, judges that "this verse should come immediately after v. 1," so that the priest and his oracle are present from the first appeal at Keilah. Wesley fills in the ephod's history: it was the high priest's ephod, holding the Urim and Thummim, "probably left in Abiathar's hand" when Ahimelech and the rest of the priests were summoned to Saul; this gave him the means both of escaping while Doeg butchered his brethren (1 Sam. 22:18) and of bringing the sacred vestment away — a treasure, Wesley adds, of which Saul was now justly deprived.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott meets the crux head-on. The whole difficulty, he says, sprang from the assumption that inquiry of the Lord could be made in no other way than through the Urim; hence the Septuagint deliberately altered the Hebrew, making Abiathar come down "with David to Keilah," as though he had joined him earlier. But the Hebrew is definite and clear: Abiathar first came to David when he was already at Keilah. The difficulty vanishes, Ellicott argues, once we remember that inquiry was not seldom made through a prophet — here through Gad — so that the earlier inquiries of vv. 2 and 4 needed no ephod at all. He points for confirmation to the later day when the Lord answered Saul "neither by dreams, nor by Urim, nor by prophets" (1 Sam. 28:6), where the three channels stand side by side.

The reformed Gill simply registers the fact that Abiathar fled to David "to Keilah, or near the place." Beneath the exegetical tug-of-war a common thread runs. All the witnesses see in Abiathar's coming a mark of God's care in the midst of David's exile: the massacre at Nob, meant to strip the sanctuary of its guardians, instead delivered the ephod and the high priest into the hands of the fugitive king, so that when David could not go up to the house of God, the choicest treasures of that house were brought down to him. Whether the earlier inquiries were made by prophet or by breastplate, from this point David has both — the living voice of Gad and the shining oracle of Abiathar — and the loss that fell on Saul became the enrichment of the man God had chosen.
7:
Saul's response to David's victory is not gratitude but a gloating misreading of providence, and the witnesses gather to expose it. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry leads. He marvels at the ingratitude: Saul heard that David had relieved Keilah, and instead of honouring him "catches at it as an opportunity of doing David a mischief." Well might David complain that his enemies rewarded him evil for good, and that for his love they were his adversaries (Ps. 35:12; 109:4); Christ himself was so used (John 10:32). Henry then names the deeper sin — Saul "abused the God of Israel," making his providence patronise a malicious design: "God hath delivered him into my hand," as though he who was rejected of God were now owned and favoured by him. Saul "vainly triumphs before the victory," forgetting how often he had had fairer advantages and missed his aim. Henry draws the lasting caution: "we must not think that one smiling providence either justifies an unrighteous cause or secures its success."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the strategic and philological texture. Saul knew there was little hope of seizing David while he roamed the open desert, but a walled town "with bars and gates," like Keilah, offered a real chance of compassing his destruction by treachery or a hand-to-hand encounter. The Hebrew, Ellicott notes, is remarkable: God has not merely delivered but "repudiated" or "rejected" him — the Septuagint renders "sold him" into Saul's hands. And he wonders that Saul could still delude himself that his was the cause of God and David the reprobate.

The reformed Gill sharpens the irony of the report: Saul was surely told not only that David had come to Keilah but why — that he had delivered the city and its harvest from the Philistines — which "one would have thought would have reconciled his mind to him"; yet it served only to feed his plot. Together the witnesses turn the verse into a study in self-deceived religion. Saul has the language of faith on his lips and the murder of a benefactor in his heart; he claps God's name upon his own vengeance and calls his prey God's gift. It is the classic temptation of the powerful — to mistake a momentary advantage for divine approval — and the classic self-condemnation of the man who was rejected precisely for such presumption. What Saul reads as God shutting David in will prove, in the next verses, to be God's own way of drawing him out.
8:
Saul summons the whole nation to arms, and the witnesses expose the pretext under which he did it. The reformed Gill notes the royal machinery: the word means Saul "caused them to hear," summoning the people by a herald sent through all his kingdom — the sovereign's prerogative to proclaim war and require attendance. The grammatical-historical commentators add the aim and the disguise. Keil and Delitzsch state the object baldly: Saul "called all the people (i.e., men of war) together to war, to go down to Keilah, and to besiege David and his men." Clarke reads it as a general levy of that region, called out "that they might scour the country, and hunt out David from all his haunts." Ellicott presses the deception: such a summons to war was indeed a royal right, but "the plea, of course, alleged for this summons was the necessity of an immediate national effort against the hereditary enemies of the people" — the Philistines. The nation is mustered against a foreign foe; the real quarry is one of its own deliverers.

Here the earlier observation of Matthew Henry comes to its point, for he had marked how Saul "abused the Israel of God, in making them the servants of his malice against David." The army that should have guarded the borders is turned into a posse for a private hatred, and its true errand is concealed, since, as the next verse will say, Saul "secretly practised mischief." The witnesses agree that this is the deeper tragedy of a tyrant: not only that some suffer under his cruelty, but that others — the whole people — are made instruments of it, marching under a false flag of patriotism to accomplish an act of personal revenge.

The contrast with the chapter's opening could not be sharper. In v. 1 the Philistines really were plundering Keilah, and it was David, with a handful of men and no commission, who went to the nation's defence. Now the king raises the entire host, names the Philistines as the enemy, and aims the whole force at the man who had just beaten them. The reader is meant to feel the inversion: the shepherd protects the flock the shepherd-king should have kept, while the king spends the flock's strength hunting the shepherd. Saul's zeal for war is real, but it is misdirected by a jealous and evil heart, and the very thoroughness of his mustering — "all the people" — measures the greatness of his obsession. The stage is set for David to learn, by the ephod, both what Saul intends and what the men of Keilah would do.
9:
David learns of the plot and calls for the ephod, and the witnesses attend both to how he knew and to what the ephod was. The reformed Gill describes the deceit: Saul "plotted and contrived" mischief, "giving out one thing, and designing another" — pretending war against the Philistines while intending to fall on Keilah and take David there. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch refines the language: David heard that Saul was "forging" mischief (the Hebrew verb elsewhere in Prov. 3:29 and 6:14), and inquired through the high priest's oracle whether the men of Keilah would betray him and whether Saul would come down; and Keil notes the method — the inquirer "placed the matter before God in prayer, and received an answer," but always to one particular question at a time.

Ellicott, of the same school, corrects a nuance of the English and opens a window on the mechanism. "Secretly," he observes, is not in the Hebrew; the sense is that Saul "was forging, or devising." And it was "likely enough that Jonathan contrived to keep his friend informed of these Court plots against him" — an intelligence that will be confirmed when the two meet in the wood. On the ephod itself Ellicott is fullest: set in it were twelve precious stones, one for each tribe, engraved with the tribal names; on solemn occasions these gems "were allowed by the providence of God to be used as oracles," their use restricted to the high priest and called out only at the bidding of the king or head of the state. The divine response, he adds, "seems to have been the visible response to earnest, faithful prayer"; the common belief was that the stones answered by some peculiar shining, though the Talmud preserves other explanations. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the scene more simply, supposing that David himself, clothed with the ephod, consulted the Lord, though he refers the reader back to the disputed question of v. 2.

The witnesses converge on the portrait of a man who, the moment danger is known, reaches not for the sword but for the oracle. "Bring hither the ephod" is, as Henry observed elsewhere, the believer's instinct — "Bring hither the Bible" — to take counsel from God in a doubtful case. Whatever the exact manner of the answer, whether by shining stone or inward suggestion, David's first act under threat is to lay the whole matter before the Lord God of Israel and wait for his word. The intelligence that Jonathan or common report had brought him is not enough; he will act only on what God discloses, and the disclosure, sobering as it proves, becomes the means of his escape.
10:
David's prayer is brief but exact, and the witnesses, few here, dwell on its reverence and its unselfishness. The reformed Gill supplies the mechanics of the scene: when the text says "Then said David," it was in fact "by the priest, for it was he that put the questions for and in the name of the inquirer." The oracle worked through Abiathar; David spoke, but the high priest presented the matter before God. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch sets the words in their place, giving the solemn address — "O Lord God of Israel, thy servant hath certainly heard that Saul seeketh to come to Keilah, to destroy the city for my sake" — as the first half of a two-part inquiry that will be completed in the verses following.

Here the reflections of Matthew Henry, gathered around this exchange, best draw out its spirit. David's address, he notes, is "very solemn and reverent": twice he calls God "the Lord God of Israel," and repeatedly styles himself "thy servant," for "those that address God must know their distance, and who they are speaking to." It is also "very particular and express" — David lays out the case with care, "for he would not call for the ephod upon every idle rumour," but only on report he had good reason to trust. Most striking is what David fears. He does not say Saul is coming "to destroy me," but "to destroy the city... for my sake." As at Nob, he dreads that his presence will bring ruin on those who shelter him; "he seems more solicitous for their safety than for his own, and will expose himself any where rather than they shall be brought into trouble by his being among them." Henry's verdict is warm: "generous souls are thus minded."

The witnesses do not differ; they let the verse stand as a model of how a servant of God brings a hard case before him. There is reverence without servility, precision without presumption, and above all a charity that thinks first of others' danger. David has just delivered Keilah; now he is willing to be delivered up rather than see Keilah suffer for harbouring him. The prayer, mediated by the priest and offered in the fear of God, asks not merely "what shall become of me?" but "what will become of them because of me?" It is the question of a shepherd, not a fugitive, and it prepares the way for the two precise inquiries — will Saul come, and will Keilah betray — that God will answer plainly, so that David may spare the city by removing himself from it.
11:
David asks two questions and receives, at first, only one answer, and this exchange draws out the chapter's richest theological reflection. The reformed Gill notes the disorder of the asking: "the questions are not orderly put... the last should have been first," which he takes as a sign of "some perturbation of mind David was in upon hearing the design of Saul against him." The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the inversion his study. In David's earnest prayer, he suggests (with Dean Payne Smith), the two questions "are put inversely to the logical order, but in accordance with their relative importance in his mind"; yet when the ephod was consulted, the replies came in logical sequence — first "He will come down," then "They will deliver thee up." Ellicott quotes the Talmud's rule that the inquirer must not ask two things at once, for he will be answered only about the first uttered; David, having framed his question out of order, received the answer as though it had been in order, and, perceiving this, repeated it properly in v. 12.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke finds here a whole doctrine of providence. This short history, he says, is "an ample proof that there is such a thing as contingency in human affairs" — that God has poised many things "between a possibility of being and not being, leaving it to the will of the creature to turn the scale." The oracle's answers carried an implied condition: if David stays, Saul will come; if Saul comes, Keilah will betray. And though the text flatly says Saul would come and the men would surrender him, neither happened — because David left. "However positive a declaration of God may appear," Clarke concludes, "the prediction is not intended to suspend or destroy free agency, but always comprehends in it some particular condition." Wesley agrees precisely: God's answer was "upon supposition" — "He purposeth to come, if thou continuest here." And Wesley adds a note on the manner of the oracle: the answer by Urim and Thummim was "not by any change in the colour or situation of the precious stones," but "by a voice or suggestion from God to the high priest" — a reading that gently parts company with the common belief, reported by Ellicott, that the stones replied by shining.

Taken together the witnesses make this verse a small treatise. God's foreknowledge reaches not only what will be but what would be under conditions that never come to pass; his warnings are real, yet they leave the creature's choice intact and are given precisely that the choice may be made rightly. David is told what Keilah would do so that he need not suffer it; the prophecy of betrayal becomes the instrument of escape. The oracle does not fetter David's freedom but informs it, and the man who inquires of God is thereby enabled to "shift for himself" under the very providence that foresaw his danger.
12:
David's second, properly ordered question — "Will the men of Keilah deliver me and my men into the hand of Saul?" — meets the plain and painful answer, "They will deliver thee up," and the witnesses draw the sting of it. The reformed Gill identifies who is meant: not the common people merely, but "the lords and great men of the place, the governor of the city, and the heads of it, the chief magistrates" — those in whose power it lay to open or hold the gates. The betrayal foretold is an act of the city's rulers, the very men whom David's sword had just preserved.

The irony is the heart of the verse, and Matthew Henry, reflecting on this whole exchange, presses the deeper marvel — that God can know such a thing at all. Had David put the question to the men of Keilah themselves, they could not have told him truly, "not knowing their own minds, nor what they should do when it came to the trial"; or they might have promised protection and yet betrayed him. "But God could tell him infallibly." Henry states the principle that governs the scene: "God knows all men better than they know themselves, knows their length, their strength, what is in them, and what they will do if they come into such and such circumstances." More than that, "He knows not only what will be, but what would be if it were not prevented; and therefore knows how to deliver the godly out of temptation."

So the two schools converge on a single, sobering lesson. The men of Keilah, at this moment grateful and admiring, "fond of David as their saviour," would nevertheless surrender him rather than face the shock of Saul's fury; their loyalty, real enough now, would not survive a siege. The verse thus lays bare the frailty of human gratitude and the reach of divine knowledge in one stroke. David is not told this to embitter him but to save him: forewarned of what even a rescued city would do under pressure, he is enabled to withdraw before the trial ever comes. What Saul had claimed in v. 7 — that God had shut David up in a walled town — God himself now overturns, using his perfect knowledge of Keilah's heart to open David's way out. The betrayal that would have doomed him becomes, once foreknown, the very reason he is spared; and the fugitive learns again that the God who searches all hearts is a surer refuge than any city's walls or any people's thanks.
13:
Forewarned, David and his men leave Keilah and go "whithersoever they could go"; Saul, hearing of the escape, gives up his march. The witnesses mark the growth of David's band, the aimlessness of his flight, and the hand of God in it. The reformed Gill counts the men: "about six hundred," an increase of two hundred since the cave of Adullam (1 Sam. 22:2), so that David "sustained no loss of men by fighting with the Philistines, but had an addition to his small forces." The grammatical-historical Ellicott confirms this as the one notice in this part of the narrative of the rapid swelling of David's ranks, and reads "whithersoever they could" as meaning that the armed camp "was pitched without any fixed plan or aim," marched in the direction of any Philistine raid, and so carried on a perpetual border warfare on Israel's behalf. Keil and Delitzsch renders the same phrase vividly — "they wandered about where they wandered about," that is, wherever they could go without danger.

Matthew Henry, whose reflections cluster around these verses, draws out the providence. David "went out, not knowing whither he went, but resolving to follow Providence and put himself under its protection." And this broke Saul's whole design: "He thought God had delivered David into his hand" — the boast of v. 7 — "but it proved that God delivered him out of his hand, as a bird out of the snare of the fowler" (Ps. 124:7). When Saul heard that David had slipped away, he "forbore to go forth" with the body of the army he had mustered, his careful plot collapsing at a single stroke.

The witnesses do not divide; together they set two movements side by side. David moves in weakness and uncertainty, with a growing but ragged force and no settled path, trusting a Providence he cannot see; Saul moves in strength and confidence, with the whole nation at his call and a sure plan, and is defeated by a rumour. The very increase of David's men — six hundred where lately there were four — quietly testifies that the God who let him be hunted was also building him an army; and the abandonment of Saul's expedition testifies that no muster of men can close a door God means to keep open. David leaves Keilah delivered and himself delivered, both by the same word from the ephod, and the chapter now follows him into the wilderness where that Providence will be tested and proved again.
14:
David settles in the wilderness strongholds of Ziph while Saul seeks him daily, and the witnesses weigh David's conduct, his hardship, and God's protecting hand. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry offers the fullest meditation. He first commends David's virtues — "his humility, modesty, fidelity to his prince, and patient attendance on the providence of his God" — for though he had the numbers to fight Saul in the field or cut him off by stratagem, "he keeps God's way, waits God's time, and is content to secure himself in woods and wildernesses." Then Henry laments David's lot: "an innocent man... terrified and put in fear of his life," a man of honour disgraced and of merit ill-requited; and he turns it heavenward — "let it make us long for that kingdom where goodness shall for ever be in glory, and holiness in honour." He names Saul's malice for what it is, "he that is born after the flesh persecuteth him that is born after the spirit" (Gal. 4:29), and rests in God's guard: unless God delivered David into Saul's hand, "he could not prevail against him" (John 19:11).

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting and press the same providential note. Keil and Delitzsch locates the desert of Ziph near Tell Zif, some way south-east of Hebron, and translates the closing clause with care: "Saul sought him all the days, but God delivered him not into his hand." Ellicott places the wilderness between Hebron and En-gedi, quotes Cowper's lines on "Judah's promised king bereft of all," and reads the last words as a general remark covering a long span: though Saul was "armed with all the power of the king in Israel, he was powerless, for the invisible King of Israel declined to give this hated David into his hand." Clarke adds only that Ziph was a city in southern Judea, near Carmel.

The reformed Gill sets the scene tersely — David in the wilderness of Ziph, "in high and strong rocks." The witnesses are wholly agreed. The verse holds together a hard providence and a sure one: Saul's tireless daily search, and God's quiet daily refusal to grant it success. David's greatness here is not in conquest but in restraint — the anointed king declining to seize by force what God had promised to give in time — and his safety is not in the rocks of Ziph but in the will of the God who governs them. That Saul sought "every day" and found nothing is the verse's whole comfort: persecution may be relentless, but it is not sovereign; the flesh may hunt the spirit, yet cannot prevail beyond the leave of heaven.
15:
David sees that Saul has come out to seek his very life, and lies hidden in a wood in the wilderness of Ziph. The witnesses treat the verse as the dark hinge that turns toward Jonathan's visit, and they attend to David's danger and to a small question of the place-name. The reformed Gill weighs how David "saw" it: either "he saw him with his bodily eyes from the top of the mountain where he was," or, more likely, "he perceived, he understood by information given him by his friends, it may be by Jonathan, or by spies he sent to observe his motions." Either way the knowledge is real and grim — Saul is now openly hunting not David's capture merely but his death.

The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the setting. Keil and Delitzsch joins this verse to the next as one period — "when David saw that Saul was come out... Jonathan rose up and went to David into the wood" — and pauses on the Hebrew word rendered "wood": it signifies a wood or thicket, though it may here be a proper name for a bushy district in the desert of Ziph where David was then stopping. He adds the traveller's melancholy note that "there is no trace of this wood now," the land having "lost its ornament of trees centuries ago through the desolating hand of man." Ellicott records the same alternative, that some understand the word as the proper name "Horesh," and confirms that "there is no trace of the wood now."

The witnesses do not quarrel; the divergence over "a wood" or "Horesh" is a matter of a place-name, not of doctrine, and both readings leave the picture intact: David crouched among thickets on a barren height, with the king of Israel and all his men abroad to take his life. The verse's whole weight falls on the phrase "to seek his life." It measures how far Saul's obsession has gone — no longer content to bar David from court or command, he means to kill him — and it measures how desolate David's condition has become, an anointed man reduced to hiding in the brush of a waterless wilderness. It is precisely at this lowest ebb, the commentators note, that help will come, and from the least expected quarter: not by force of arms or a turn of fortune, but by the arrival of a friend who will "strengthen his hand in God." The gloom of v. 15 is the setting against which the grace of v. 16 shines.
16:
At David's darkest hour Jonathan comes to him in the wood and "strengthened his hand in God," and the witnesses make this the moral centre of the chapter. The grammatical-historical commentators frame the visit. Keil and Delitzsch notes that after his encouraging words the two "made a covenant before Jehovah," renewing by a fresh solemn oath the bond already made, after which "Jonathan returned home, but David remained in the wood"; and he sets the scene in sharp relief — "the treachery of the Ziphites forms a striking contrast to Jonathan's treatment of David." Ellicott dwells on the psychology. The phrase "strengthened his hand in God," he says, is added by the narrator "to show how sorely tried was the king of the future at this juncture." The relentless hostility of Saul, once his friend, "the apparent hopelessness of his struggle," the cruel ingratitude of whole bodies of his countrymen like the men of Ziph, his "homeless, outlawed condition" — all these "naturally weighed upon the nervous and enthusiastic temperament of David, which was soon depressed," and are "breathed forth in not a few of the Psalms." Against this darkness, Jonathan's "warm sympathy, the steady onlook to a sunnier future... was a real help to David."

The wesleyan-arminian voices dwell on how the strengthening was done. Wesley says Jonathan "comforted and supported him against all his fears, by minding him of God's infallible promises made to him, and his singular providence which hitherto had and still would be with him." Clarke adds the constitutional and moral vindication of Jonathan's act. There was, he supposes, "always a secret intercourse between David and Jonathan," by which David was warned of Saul's designs; and Jonathan, knowing "that God had appointed David to the kingdom," came now "to encourage him to trust in the Most High." All this was consistent with his duty to father and king, for "David had delivered the kingdom," Saul "was ruling unconstitutionally," and God had fixed the succession — yet neither friend "took one step to hasten the time." Clarke's verdict is unstinting: "a more filial and a more loyal part he could not have acted."

The witnesses do not divide; they unite in admiration. Jonathan strengthens David not with troops he could not spare nor with a rescue he could not manage, but "in God" — turning the hunted man's eyes back to the promise, the anointing, and the providence that had kept him thus far. It is the highest office of friendship, the commentators imply: not to remove a trial, which was beyond Jonathan's power, but to fortify the heart to bear it, so that the outlaw might "face the grave difficulties of his dangerous position" with new courage. That the prince who might have envied David instead braved his father's court to hearten him is the wonder the witnesses will unfold further in the verse that follows.
17:
Jonathan's words of comfort — "Fear not... thou shalt be king over Israel, and I shall be next unto thee; and that also Saul my father knoweth" — draw from the witnesses a study in astonishing humility and in the pathos of a last farewell. The reformed Gill unfolds the opening charge: "Fear not" means "distrust not the power, providence, and promises of God, nor dread the wrath of Saul, or fear falling into his hands." The exile is bidden to rest his whole confidence on the God who had marked him for a throne.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott lingers on "I shall be next unto thee," finding in it a "pathetic interest of its own." To readers who know that this generous friend would soon "find a grave on Mount Gilboa instead of a home with David," the words are heavy with sorrow. Had Jonathan lived, Ellicott judges, "he would have certainly ceded any rights he had to the throne of his father Saul in favour of David," unlike the rival Ishbosheth; but "his generosity was not to be exposed to any such severe test," and David was spared so gifted a rival. On "Saul my father knoweth," Ellicott infers that by now the secret of Samuel's anointing of David had likely become known at court, so that "very many in Israel looked on him as the anointed successor of Saul." He marks the solemn finality of the meeting: "after this meeting David never looked on Jonathan's face in life again."

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley catches the self-abnegation exactly. Jonathan gathered his hope of the second place "either from David's generosity... or from some promise made to him by David," and the whole import is this: "I do not look to be king myself (as by my birth I might expect), but... thou shalt be king (God having so appointed) and I but in a secondary place inferior to thee." The witnesses are of one mind, and their wonder is unfeigned. Here is the heir apparent of Israel, standing before the hunted man whom God has chosen over him, and not merely accepting his own displacement but rejoicing in it — content to be "next," to serve where by birth he might have reigned. It is faith's triumph over nature and ambition: Jonathan bows to the divine appointment as gladly as he loves the man appointed. And the verse gains its tender weight from what the reader knows and Jonathan did not — that the throne he ceded he would never live to stand beside, and that this brave, believing surrender was spoken at their final parting. The friendship is sealed, in the next verse, by a covenant before the Lord.
18:
The two friends "made a covenant before the Lord," after which David remained in the wood and Jonathan went home; and the witnesses, though few, weigh the solemnity and the sorrow of the act. The reformed Gill reads it as a renewal: they "renewed the covenant they had before made in the name and fear of God, and before him as a witness of it," and he notes that Kimchi and Abarbinel comment on the phrase. The bond was not new but re-sworn, its old obligations freshly confirmed in the divine presence. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating this with the verses before, likewise calls it a covenant "before Jehovah," a renewing "by another solemn oath" of the friendship already sealed, before Jonathan returned to his house and David abode in the wood.

Here Matthew Henry's reflection on this friendship draws out its spirit. As "a constant friend," Jonathan "renewed his league of friendship" with David, and Henry finds in it a pattern: "true love takes delight in repeating its engagements, giving and receiving fresh assurances of the firmness of the friendship." From this he lifts a devotional lesson — "our covenant with God should be often renewed, and therein our communion with him kept up." The repeated oath is not distrust but the joy of love confirming itself.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds the note that gives the verse its ache: "they then parted, and never came together again, that we find, in this world." The witnesses hold these two things together — the sacredness of the covenant and the finality of the parting. Jonathan will go back to his father's doomed house; David will stay among the thickets of Ziph; and though they swear an eternal friendship before God, their eyes will not meet again on earth. Yet the commentators find no futility in it, for the covenant was struck "before the Lord," and what is committed to him is not lost by death. The scene closes a movement that began in David's despair: a friend came, strengthened his hand in God, ceded him a throne, and bound himself to him by oath — and then withdrew, leaving David alone again but no longer downcast. The very next verse will show how quickly the wilderness turns hostile once more, as the men of Ziph, who should have sheltered their own tribesman, go up to betray him; but the friend's covenant, sealed in God's presence, stands over the whole dark sequel as a pledge that love and the promise will outlast the treachery.
19:
The men of Ziph go up to Saul at Gibeah and offer to betray David's hiding-place, and the witnesses read their treachery against the friendship just recorded. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the outrage in full light. David was "sheltering himself in the wilderness of Ziph... putting the more confidence in the people of that country because they were of his own tribe," and they "had reason to think themselves happy" to serve "the ornament of their tribe," who far from plundering them was ready to protect them. Instead, "to ingratiate themselves with Saul, they went to him," told him exactly where David quartered, and invited him to come and take his prey — and this, Henry notes with disgust, "without asking a reward (as Judas did — What will you give me?)." The grammatical-historical Ellicott confirms the portrait: the Ziphites were "very warm adherents of Saul, entirely devoted to his fortunes," who "kept their promise faithfully" and brought David into "extreme danger of capture." He clears up the geography, explaining that "Jeshimon" is not a place-name but a word for "desert" or "solitude" (Isa. 43:19), naming the barren white-chalk waste between the Dead Sea and the Hebron mountains — "the wilderness of Judea" of Matt. 3:1. Keil and Delitzsch adds the bitter sequel that for this betrayal "Saul blessed them."

The reformed Gill notes that the Ziphites, though of David's own tribe, were "terrified with what Saul had done to Nob" and so thought it safest to inform on him; and he records that at this time David wrote a psalm of complaint. Here the witnesses touch a small divergence: Gill assigns it to the fifty-ninth Psalm, while the wesleyan-arminian Wesley — with Henry — refers David's cry to the fifty-fourth, in which he calls the Ziphites "strangers" (Ps. 54:3), "though they were Israelites, because they used him barbarously." The difference is one of reference, not of substance.

What unites the witnesses is the moral shock of the scene. Set beside Jonathan — a prince who braved his father to strengthen a fugitive not of his own blood — the Ziphites are kinsmen who sell their tribesman and benefactor to buy a tyrant's favour. The contrast Keil drew is the verse's whole point: unbought love from one who had everything to lose, unbidden treachery from those who had everything to gain by loyalty. David is thrust from the shelter he trusted, driven deeper into the desert; yet the very psalm born of this betrayal turns the wrong Godward — "Behold, God is my helper" — so that the Ziphites' malice, like Saul's, becomes an occasion for the fugitive's faith.
20:
The Ziphites press their offer with fawning eagerness — "Now therefore, O king, come down according to all the desire of thy soul... and our part shall be to deliver him into the king's hand" — and the witnesses catch both the flattery and its self-condemning phrase. The reformed Gill marks the plain movement of it: they invite Saul to "come down from Gibeah to Ziph, and the wilderness of it, where David was." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch renders the Hebrew closely to bring out its obsequious force: "according to all the desire of thy soul, O king, to come down, come down, and it is in us to deliver him into the hand of the king." The doubled "come down" and the confident "it is in us" together paint informers who have measured the king's hunger and hasten to feed it.

The revealing words are "all the desire of thy soul." As Matthew Henry observed of this whole approach, the Ziphites "invited him to come with his forces into their country in pursuit of him, and promised to deliver him into his hand." They do not merely report David's whereabouts; they undertake his capture, pledging themselves as Saul's guides and agents. And in framing their promise around the king's soul's desire they unwittingly expose what that desire is — not the defence of the realm, not justice, but the destruction of one innocent man. They flatter Saul by naming his obsession as though it were a worthy longing to be gratified.

The witnesses let the verse stand as a portrait of malice made serviceable. The Ziphites furnish what Saul most wants — local knowledge, a guarantee of delivery, and the moral cover of loyal subjects doing their king a service. Their zeal is entirely unbidden; Saul had not sent to threaten or bribe them, yet they come of their own accord, competing to betray. It is the way of a corrupt court that the powerful never lack instruments: where a king's heart is set on evil, there are always those who will call his cruelty his "desire" and offer their hands to accomplish it. Against this the reader remembers Jonathan, who used his knowledge of Saul's designs to save David, while the Ziphites use their knowledge of David's hiding to destroy him. The same information serves love in one man and treachery in others; the difference lies wholly in the heart. Saul's grateful blessing, in the very next verse, will show how welcome such servants are to a soul bent on blood, and how readily wickedness clothes itself in the language of piety and thanks.
21:
Saul receives the Ziphites' offer with a blessing — "Blessed be ye of the Lord; for ye have compassion on me" — and the witnesses fasten on the shocking piety of a murderer's thanks. The reformed Gill takes the words at their surface: Saul "highly commends them for the offer they made to him, blesses God for them, and desires the blessing of God upon them for it." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch probes beneath. "For this treachery Saul blessed them," he writes; and he traces the perverse feeling that made it possible — "in his evil conscience he suspected David of seeking to become his murderer, and therefore thanked God in his delusion that the Ziphites had had compassion upon him, and shown him David's hiding-place." Saul's gratitude is the fruit of a guilty imagination that has turned the victim into the aggressor.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry is sharpest of all on the hypocrisy. "Blessed be you of the Lord," he quotes, then cuts: "so near is God to his mouth, though far from his heart." Saul "looked upon himself as a miserable man and an object of pity," when in truth "his own envy and ill-nature made him so; otherwise he might have been easy and have needed no man's compassion." And in the words "you have compassion on me" Henry hears a sidelong complaint against his people — "which others have not." Elsewhere Henry draws the abiding warning from this same false devotion: "in the midst of his wickedness, Saul affected to speak the language of piety," and "such expressions, without suitable effects, can only amuse or deceive those who hear, and those who use them."

The witnesses converge on a single verdict: here is religion emptied of righteousness. Saul can name the Lord, bless in his name, and give thanks for a mercy — and the mercy for which he thanks God is help toward shedding innocent blood. The name of God is on his lips while its fear is absent from his heart. The commentators find in it both a diagnosis and a caution. The diagnosis: a bad conscience will invert reality, casting the persecutor as the persecuted, so that the hunter pities himself and thanks heaven for his prey. The caution: pious words are no proof of a pious soul, for the worst of men may speak them fluently, and do, when it serves their turn. Saul's blessing of the Ziphites stands as the counterfeit of true thanksgiving, and it prepares the reader to see, in the verses to come, how a self-deceived heart can pursue its sin with the language of devotion until the very providence it presumes upon turns it aside.
22:
Saul, still anxious lest his prey escape, sends the Ziphites back to gather surer intelligence — "know and see his place where his haunt is, and who hath seen him there: for it is told me that he dealeth very subtilly" — and the witnesses mark both his caution and its irony. The reformed Gill reads the command practically: Saul bids them "go home, return to their habitations, and get things in a greater readiness for him; inquire more diligently after David, get more intelligence of him, and inform themselves more exactly about him." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch catches the note of fear beneath the order: "in his anxiety, however, lest David should escape him after all, he charged them" to give still further heed, to "reconnoitre and look at his place where his foot cometh," and to learn "who hath seen him there," lest Saul "be deceived by uncertain or false reports."

The pointed word is Saul's own: David "dealeth very subtilly." As Matthew Henry observed of this charge, Saul represents David "as a man crafty to do mischief, whereas all his subtlety was to secure himself." Herein lies the verse's quiet indictment. The king projects his own designing mind onto the man he hunts: he who "secretly practised mischief" now credits David with dangerous cunning, and reads the fugitive's mere efforts at self-preservation as the schemes of a wily enemy. The guilty conscience of v. 21, which had cast David as a would-be murderer, here casts him as a master of subtlety — a threat so elusive that even willing informers must double their diligence before the king will risk the field.

The witnesses draw the scene without dispute, and it repays notice on two counts. First, it shows the labour that hatred will spend: Saul does not rush down at the first report but organizes a careful reconnaissance, wanting certainty before he strikes, so bent is he on not missing again. Second, it shows how sin misnames its object. David's "subtlety" is nothing but the caution of a hunted man who moves from thicket to rock to save his life; yet to Saul's fear it looks like craft, and the innocent evasions of the persecuted are branded as the arts of a schemer. Providence, meanwhile, is using this very delay — Saul's insistence on further intelligence rather than immediate pursuit — to give David time to shift his ground, so that the king's excess of caution, meant to make his prey certain, becomes one more means of the prey's escape.
23:
Saul charges the Ziphites to map every one of David's lurking-places and bring him certain word, vowing to "search him out throughout all the thousands of Judah," and the witnesses attend to his thoroughness and to the tribal word he uses. The reformed Gill reads the command as a demand for exact reconnaissance — that they take note of "all the lurking places where he hideth himself... which he most frequents, that ye may be able to describe them, and the way to them." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gives the whole of Saul's resolve: "come ye again to me with the certainty... that I may go with you; and if he is in the land, I will search him out among all the thousands (i.e., families) of Judah." Ellicott glosses the term precisely: the "thousands" (Hebrew alaphim) were "the greater tribal divisions" (Num. 1:16; 10:4), and Judah is "especially mentioned by Saul as being the tribe of David," where he found "probably the larger number of his adherents" and which was "the typical tribe" in southern Canaan.

The verse discloses the reach of Saul's determination. He will not merely follow a single report but comb the entire tribal territory, family by family, if only he can first be certain of the ground. As Matthew Henry noted of this stage, Saul now "thought himself sure of his prey and pleased himself with the thoughts of devouring it," having, as he supposed, laid his informers upon every likely place. The whole apparatus of the state — the king in person, his army, and a network of local guides — is bent upon one hunted man.

The witnesses let a bitter irony stand unspoken but plain. It is "the thousands of Judah," David's own tribe, that Saul proposes to ransack for him; the very people from whom the future king should have drawn his surest loyalty are to be turned into the field of his search. The Ziphites have already shown how a tribesman may betray; Saul now assumes that all Judah may be searched with the same cooperation. Yet the reader knows what Saul does not — that no comb of "all the thousands of Judah" can find a man whom the invisible King of Israel has resolved to keep. Saul's confidence is thorough, methodical, and doomed; his very care to be certain before he strikes will cost him the interval in which David slips away to Maon, and beyond his reach. The verse thus measures both the greatness of the danger closing on David and the emptiness of a determination that reckons without God.
24:
The Ziphites hasten home ahead of Saul, while David and his men move on to the wilderness of Maon, "in the plain on the south of Jeshimon," and the witnesses give chiefly the geography and the reason it was recorded. The reformed Gill clears up "before Saul": they went "not before his person, as if he went with them," but "before he went, to prepare things more exactly... and in order to return to him again and go with him." The Ziphites are the advance party; the king will follow. The grammatical-historical commentators locate the new refuge. Keil and Delitzsch places Maon (now Main) some three and three-quarter hours south-south-east of Hebron, only two hours from Ziph and visible from it, and identifies "the plain" as "the Arabah or steppe" to the right of the wilderness. Ellicott sets it "still further to the south," its name "still preserved in the village or small town of Main, which is built on a prominent conical hill."

Ellicott alone pauses to ask why so exact a topography stands in the sacred text, and his answer opens a window on Israel's love for this history. The precise description, he suggests, "was, no doubt, inserted by the compiler of these books, owing to the intense interest which the wanderings of this favourite hero and king excited among his countrymen." He imagines later generations, after "the glorious reign of David" had made Israel "a great and renowned nation," gladly tracing "the itinerary of the great king as he fled for his life before Saul," each dry place-name having once been "the resort of reverent and curious pilgrims, anxious to gaze on localities made sacred by the weary wanderings and the hair-breadth escapes of the glorious king of Israel."

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is a way-mark, and they are content to plant it accurately on the map. Yet in Ellicott's reflection it gains a quiet significance. What was, at the moment, only one more forced retreat of a hunted outlaw — driven from Ziph to Maon, ever deeper into the parched Arabah — became, in the memory of the people, a sacred trail, because the fugitive was the man after God's own heart and the throne toward which God was leading him lay on the far side of these sufferings. The plain south of Jeshimon is where the noose draws tightest, for Maon is where Saul will nearly close his hand upon David; and the very exactness with which the escape is charted testifies that Israel long treasured the memory of how narrowly, and how providentially, their king was preserved. The stage is now set, on this visible conical hill, for the closest call of the whole pursuit.
25:
Saul and his men set out to seek David, who, being warned, "came down into a rock" and stayed in the wilderness of Maon while Saul pressed the pursuit. The witnesses supply the topography and a small question about the "rock." The reformed Gill notes simply that "Saul with his army came out in search of him," whether newly informed by the Ziphites or not. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identifies the feature: "the rock" is "probably the conical mountain of Main (Maon), the top of which is now surrounded with ruins," for it "can only have been the mountain" along one side of which David would presently pass while Saul went along the other. On being told of Saul's approach, David "went down the rock, and remained in the desert of Maon." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke adds that Maon "was a mountainous district in the most southern parts of Judah," which Calmet would connect with a city near Gaza.

Clarke's fellow Wesleyan, Wesley, offers a slight refinement of the scene: "a rock," he says, means "a cave which was in the rock," where David "at first might think to hide himself, but upon farther consideration he removed from thence upon Saul's approach." Whether we picture David lodged on a rocky height or withdrawing from a cave within it, the witnesses agree on the essential movement — the fugitive taking to the crags of Maon as Saul's forces come on.

The verse, spare as it is, carries the tightening of the chase and the note that will sound through David's psalms. Again and again in his songs the hunted man will call God his "rock," his "fortress," his "high tower"; and here the figure is literal — he goes down into a rock to save his life. Yet the commentators, by their very sobriety, let the reader feel that the stone of Maon is not finally what shelters him. David has fled to the best cover the wilderness affords, and it will not be enough: the next verse will show Saul and his men so near that only a single mountain divides them, and David "making haste to get away for fear." The rock buys time, not safety; the deliverance, when it comes, will come not from the strength of the hills but from a messenger sent in God's providence to call Saul away. So the scene is poised — David in the rock, Saul closing in — and everything now waits on the God who alone can turn the pursuer aside.
26:
The pursuit reaches its crisis: Saul on one side of the mountain, David and his men on the other, David "making haste to get away for fear of Saul," while Saul's men close a ring to seize them. The witnesses render the peril and then the providence. The reformed Gill draws the picture plainly — Saul's army "came to the very mountain where David and his men were, the one was on one side of it, and the other on the other side; there was only one mountain between them." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch heightens it: "David was anxiously concerned to escape from Saul, and Saul and his men were encircling David and his men to seize them"; David "had thus almost inextricably fallen into the hands of Saul," and only the arrival of a messenger, in God's timing, saved him. Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, adds the human touch that Saul pressed along his side of the mountain "evidently not knowing that David and his men were on the other side," so blindly did the hunter grope after his prey.

It is Matthew Henry who lifts the mountain into a symbol. There was "but a mountain between them," David and his men fleeing on one side, Saul and his men pursuing on the other, "David in fear and Saul in hope." "But this mountain," Henry writes, "was an emblem of the divine Providence coming between David and the destroyer, like the pillar of cloud between the Israelites and the Egyptians. David was concealed by this mountain and Saul confounded by it." So "David now flees as a bird to his mountain" (Ps. 11:1) and "finds God to him as the shadow of a great rock."

The witnesses hold together the two truths the verse presses upon them. On the one side, the danger is at its extremity — no other passage in the chapter brings Saul so near, a single ridge from success, his ring all but closed. On the other, that very ridge is God's chosen instrument of rescue: what looks like the last thin barrier before capture is in fact the wall Providence has set between the destroyer and the destined king. The commentators are careful not to make the rock itself the saviour; the mountain hides, but only God confounds, and the deliverance will arrive not from the terrain but from the messenger of the next verse. Yet they let the emblem stand as comfort for every believer brought to such a strait: when the pursuer is one ridge away and the net drawing tight, the God who set a cloud between Israel and Egypt can set a mountain between his servant and ruin, and hold the enemy blind and baffled on the far slope until the way of escape is opened.
27:
At the instant of David's greatest peril a messenger reaches Saul — "Haste thee, and come; for the Philistines have invaded the land" — and the witnesses see in the timing the visible hand of God. The reformed Gill notes that the messenger came "from his court, by order of his council there," adds that "the Jews say it was an angel from heaven," and concludes that, angel or man, "it was certainly the providence of God that directed this affair, that a messenger should come to Saul just at that very time that David was like to fall into his hands." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke presses the marvel hardest. "See the providence of God exerted for the salvation of David's life!" David and his men were "almost surrounded... and on the point of being taken, when a messenger arrives." And Clarke traces the double working of that providence: God "had already prepared the invasion of the land by the Philistines, and kept Saul ignorant how much David was in his power," so that the news arrived "just at the point of time to prevent" the capture that Saul's scouts must otherwise have made within moments. "Here David was delivered by God, and in such a manner too as rendered the Divine interposition visible."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott confirms the reading and supplies the mechanism, citing Lange that this was "God's plan to save David": the Philistines had "probably availed themselves of the opportunity which Saul's withdrawal of his forces southward" had given them, and were "invading in force the more northern provinces." Wesley states the lesson in a sentence: "The wisdom of God is never at a loss for ways and means to preserve his people."

The witnesses do not divide; they marvel together. Every strand of the deliverance is woven of ordinary threads — a border raid, a court messenger, a king's duty to his realm — yet the pattern is unmistakably God's. The very obsession that had drawn Saul and his whole force far to the south, leaving the frontier bare, is what opened the land to the Philistines; and the Philistine attack, foreknown and even "prepared" by God, is what now tears Saul away at the one moment when his hand was all but on David. There is an irony the commentators relish: the Philistines whom David had beaten at Keilah in the chapter's opening become, all unknowing, the instruments of David's rescue at its close. So the God who would not give David into Saul's hand keeps his word not by thunder from heaven but by the timing of events, delivering his servant "in such a manner as rendered the Divine interposition visible," that David and every later reader might know who it was that stood between him and death.
28:
Saul breaks off the pursuit to face the Philistines, and the place is named Sela-hammahlekoth in memory of the escape; the witnesses agree on the deliverance but divide over what the name means. The reformed Gill marks the abruptness of the rescue: Saul "stopped short at once, as soon as ever he received the message," turning from David to the invaders. The name given to the rock preserves the wonder of the moment, and here the commentators part company.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads it "the rock of divisions," and offers two grounds: the Targum's, "because the heart of the king was divided to go hither and thither," torn between his hunt and his kingdom; and the plain historical fact that "here Saul was obliged to separate himself from David, in order to go and oppose the invading Philistines." The grammatical-historical voices prefer another root. Ellicott grants that the margin renders it "the rock (or cliff) of divisions," but judges that "other scholars, with greater reason, prefer the derivation from a Hebrew word signifying to be smooth — the cliff of smoothness: that is, of slipping away or escaping." Keil and Delitzsch agrees and argues the point: "rock of smoothnesses, i.e., of slipping away or escaping," is "better supported than rock of divisions," since the Hebrew root "does not mean to separate." On this reading the name commemorates not Saul's divided errand but David's slipping free.

Ellicott records a third proposal as well, noting that "Ewald rather fancifully interprets the term as the Cliff of Destiny or of Fate" — a reading the witnesses mention only to set aside as a flight of speculation beyond the plain sense.

The debate, though philological, is not empty, for each rendering fixes the memory on a different actor. "Rock of divisions" looks to Saul — his heart pulled two ways, his forced separation from his prey. "Rock of escaping" looks to David — the smooth slope down which he slipped from a closing hand. Yet under the dispute the witnesses are wholly one on the substance. Whatever the etymology, the place was named because at this rock, with a single mountain between the king and his victim and the ring all but shut, God turned the pursuer aside and set his servant free. The stone became a standing memorial, its very name — however construed — a witness that the deliverance was worth remembering; and the reader who cannot finally decide between "division" and "escape" may hold both, for it was by dividing Saul from his purpose that God wrought David's escape. The chapter's long pursuit ends not in capture but in a named rock, a monument to the providence that had refused, all through, to give David into Saul's hand.
29:
David leaves Maon and takes up his new refuge in the strongholds of En-gedi, and the witnesses, closing the chapter, mark both the narrowness of the escape and the character of the place. The reformed Gill reads the departure against the crisis just past: David "went up from thence, from the wilderness of Maon, having had a narrow escape for his life." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply records the move — David "went up from thence, and dwelt in strong holds at Engedi" — carrying the fugitive to firmer ground. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke supplies the fullest description of the site: En-gedi lay "near to the western coast of the Dead Sea, not far from Jeshimon"; the name "literally signifies the kid's well," and the region was "celebrated for its vineyards" (Song 1:14) and its balm — "mountainous, filled with caverns, and consequently proper for David in his present circumstances."

Matthew Henry, closing his exposition, adds a devotional note drawn from a later psalm. He follows Dr. Lightfoot in taking this to be the wilderness of Judah where David composed the sixty-third Psalm (Ps. 63:1) — a song that "breathes as much pious and devout affection as almost any of his psalms; for in all places and in all conditions he still kept up his communion with God." Thus the caverns of En-gedi, which sheltered David's body, witnessed also the continued worship of his soul.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they let the chapter end on a note of settled providence. David has been driven from Keilah to Ziph, from Ziph to Maon, and now from Maon to En-gedi — ever deeper into the wilderness, yet never into Saul's hand. The rocky, cavern-riddled coast of the Dead Sea is exactly the ground a hunted man needs, and the God who had refused all through the chapter to give David up now grants him this stronger fastness in which to breathe again. Yet the commentators are careful, in Henry's closing word, to lift the reader's eye above the caves to the communion they sheltered: David's true stronghold was not the crags of En-gedi but the God to whom he still sang in the wilderness. The chapter that began with an act of public deliverance ends with a private one — the anointed man preserved once more — and the vineyards and springs of the kid's well close the account with a quiet promise that the fugitive's long flight is still watched over, and that the God who kept him will bring him at last to the throne toward which these very wanderings were leading.
